dev_plans.content:
LIGHTWEIGHT / HEURISTIC APPROACH — Correlation-matrix discrepancy.

WHAT IS MEASURED: The gap between the attribute co-occurrence structure the model *predicts* and the co-occurrence structure actually present in the ground-truth labels. If the model is exploiting shortcuts (e.g. inferring Heavy_Makeup from Male), its predicted attributes will be MORE inter-correlated than the labels justify; the excess correlation is the shortcut signal.

WHERE IN PIPELINE: Inserted as a post-validation hook that runs once at the end of every epoch, on the held-out CelebA validation split, immediately after the standard val-loss/F1 evaluation. Uses the same forward passes already done for validation (no extra training cost beyond storing predictions).

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. During the per-epoch validation loop, accumulate two matrices over all N val images and the K=40 attributes: collect the model's sigmoid outputs thresholded at 0.5 into a binary prediction matrix P (N x K), and the ground-truth label matrix Y (N x K).
2. Compute Pearson correlation matrices C_pred = corrcoef(P^T) and C_label = corrcoef(Y^T), each K x K. Use float predictions (not just binary) for C_pred to reduce quantization noise; clamp NaNs (constant columns) to 0.
3. For every off-diagonal pair (i,j), compute the *excess* correlation e_ij = max(0, |C_pred[i,j]| - |C_label[i,j]|). Excess (not signed diff) is used because shortcut learning specifically INFLATES correlation; predicting weaker-than-label correlation is not a shortcut concern.
4. Metric = mean of e_ij over all K*(K-1)/2 unique off-diagonal pairs.
5. Also log the top-10 most-inflated pairs to a text file each epoch for human inspection, and dump C_pred / C_label as a heatmap PNG (matplotlib imshow, diverging colormap) every 5 epochs.

OUTPUT/ARTIFACT: a single float per epoch (mean excess correlation) written to the metrics CSV/JSON time series; a per-epoch top-inflated-pairs log; periodic correlation-heatmap PNGs. An alert is raised if the metric trends upward while val-loss falls (classic shortcut signature: getting 'better' by leaning harder on proxies).
dev_plans.metric: Mean excess predicted-vs-label attribute correlation on the validation set per epoch (mean over all 780 attribute pairs of max(0, |corr_pred| - |corr_label|)); lower is better.
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.04 — a carefully trained CelebA multi-label CNN tracks the true label correlation structure closely; published well-tuned baselines reproduce attribute co-occurrence to within a few hundredths, so residual inflation below ~0.04 indicates predictions are driven by genuine per-attribute signal rather than proxies.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.08 — a decently-trained model typically still over-couples a handful of gender-linked attribute pairs, pushing mean excess into the 0.05–0.08 band before diminishing returns; we'd settle here.

dev_plans.content:
STATISTICALLY RIGOROUS APPROACH — Worst-group accuracy on decorrelated / counterfactual subsets (group-robustness, after Sagawa et al.).

WHAT IS MEASURED: How well the model performs specifically on ATYPICAL attribute combinations — the cases where a shortcut (proxy) and the true target disagree. A shortcut-reliant model scores high on common combos but collapses on rare counterfactual ones; worst-group accuracy exposes exactly this fragility.

WHERE IN PIPELINE: Run once per epoch on a pre-computed, fixed set of 'decorrelated groups' carved out of the validation split. Group definitions are computed ONCE before training and cached.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Pre-processing (once): pick a small set of target/spurious attribute pairs known to be strongly correlated in CelebA — e.g. (Heavy_Makeup | Male), (Wearing_Lipstick | Male), (5_o_Clock_Shadow | Male), (Blond_Hair | Male). For each target t with spurious s, partition validation images into 4 groups by (t value, s value). The minority/counterfactual groups (e.g. Male=1 & Heavy_Makeup=1, or Male=0 & 5_o_Clock_Shadow=1) are the shortcut-stress groups.
2. Each epoch, run the model over the validation set, and for each defined target attribute compute per-group accuracy of that attribute's prediction within each of its 4 groups.
3. For each target, take the MINIMUM accuracy across its 4 groups (worst-group accuracy). 
4. Metric = mean of these worst-group accuracies across all selected target attributes. (Averaging keeps it a single stable float; also log each target's worst-group value individually.)
5. To make it statistically sound, only include groups with >= 30 samples; report a 95% Wilson confidence interval per group in the side log so noisy tiny groups can be down-weighted in interpretation.

OUTPUT/ARTIFACT: one float per epoch (mean worst-group accuracy) in the time-series; a per-target worst-group breakdown table per epoch; a bar chart per 5 epochs showing each group's accuracy + CI. The gap between overall accuracy and worst-group accuracy is logged as a secondary 'shortcut gap' diagnostic. Higher worst-group accuracy = less shortcut reliance.
dev_plans.metric: Mean worst-group accuracy across the selected spurious-correlated target attributes, evaluated on fixed decorrelated validation groups, per epoch; higher is better.
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.80 — group-robustness work on CelebA (e.g. Group-DRO) reports worst-group accuracies in the low-to-mid 80s for well-regularized models on spurious-correlation tasks; reaching 0.80 means counterfactual combos are handled almost as well as typical ones.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.68 — standard ERM training on CelebA commonly lands worst-group accuracy in the high 60s before explicit robustness intervention, so 0.68 is the realistic soft target under plain-training constraints.

dev_plans.content:
TOOLING / LIBRARY-BASED APPROACH — Conditional mutual-information shortcut score via scikit-learn.

WHAT IS MEASURED: Whether the model's prediction for a target attribute carries information about a spurious attribute that is NOT already explained by the true target label — i.e. residual dependence on the proxy. Operationalized as the drop in predictive dependence between prediction and spurious attribute after conditioning on the true target, using mutual-information estimators from scikit-learn (sklearn.metrics.mutual_info_score / sklearn.feature_selection.mutual_info_classif).

WHERE IN PIPELINE: Post-validation hook each epoch, on the validation split, reusing stored predictions. Leverages scikit-learn so no bespoke statistics code is needed; optionally cross-checked with dython's nominal-association utilities.

STEP-BY-STEP:
1. Each epoch, after validation inference, build arrays for each (target t, spurious s) pair from the standard CelebA-correlated set: pred_t (binary model prediction of t), y_t (true label of t), y_s (true label of spurious s).
2. For each pair, estimate I(pred_t ; y_s) and the conditional dependence: compute I(pred_t ; y_s | y_t) by stratifying on y_t (split into y_t=0 and y_t=1 subsets, compute mutual_info_score(pred_t, y_s) within each, weight-average by subset size). Conditioning on the true target removes the legitimate label-driven correlation, leaving only shortcut dependence.
3. Normalize each conditional MI by the entropy of y_s (sklearn) to get a 0–1 normalized shortcut score per pair.
4. Metric = mean normalized conditional MI across all evaluated pairs. A value near 0 means once the true attribute is known the prediction tells you nothing extra about the proxy (no shortcut); larger values reveal proxy leakage.
5. Log per-pair scores; produce a per-5-epoch bar chart ranking the worst leaking pairs. Optionally validate the estimator against a permutation null (shuffle y_s) to get a noise floor printed in the log.

OUTPUT/ARTIFACT: one float per epoch (mean normalized conditional MI) in the metrics time-series; per-pair leakage table; ranked bar chart every 5 epochs; permutation-null reference line. Lower is better.
dev_plans.metric: Mean normalized conditional mutual information I(pred_target ; spurious_attr | true_target) across the selected correlated attribute pairs on the validation set, per epoch; lower is better.
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.03 — for a model relying on genuine visual evidence, conditioning on the true label should leave near-zero residual MI with the proxy; estimator noise on validation-sized samples sits around a few hundredths, so ≤0.03 indicates no meaningful shortcut leakage.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.07 — typical ERM-trained CelebA models retain modest proxy leakage on gender-linked pairs, landing mean normalized conditional MI in the 0.04–0.07 range before extra debiasing is needed.
